topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure, slick and aggressively optimistic, lay crumpled on the kitchen counter. I stared at the architectural renderings—all sharp angles, minimalist glass, and sterile white marble. My stomach tightened into a cold, hard knot. "Condominium conversion," the email had stated, a phrase that tasted like ash in my mouth. I traced the outline of the old, familiar fire escape on my window, a relic of a time when things felt solid. Every time I looked at the building, I saw not home, but a future stripped bare, an erasure of everything that had made this place bearable. I scrubbed at the already clean counter, the harsh sound of the sponge against the granite mirroring the grating sensation rising in my chest. I couldn't bring myself to look at the realtor’s website again; the sheer audacity of it made my jaw ache.

[story 2]

"So, we're turning this into luxury units?" I asked, my voice coming out thin and reedy, a sound I barely recognized. The developer’s pitch had been a relentless wave of polished jargon, and now, standing in the lobby, surrounded by assistants in crisp, impersonal suits, the reality hit me like a physical blow. My hands clenched so tightly around my tote bag that my knuckles turned white. I kept glancing at the faded wallpaper in the hallway, the patterns mocking the sleek, future-forward plans plastered everywhere. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the air feel thick and hard to draw in. I just wanted to retreat, to find a corner where the veneer of corporate ambition couldn't touch me.

[story 3]

I walked through the common area, deliberately avoiding eye contact with the cheerful, vacant smiles of the leasing agents. The air smelled faintly of new paint and expensive cleaning solutions, a scent that felt profoundly alien. Each polished surface reflected back a distorted version of myself—a shadow stretched and unfamiliar. I stopped by the elevator bank, pressing my palm against the cool brushed steel. There was a profound sense of wrongness settling over me, a deep-seated refusal to accept this transformation. I pulled my hand away quickly, as if burned. "This isn't right," I muttered, the words barely audible above the ambient, synthesized music. I needed space, quiet, anything to buffer the relentless, unsettling feeling that this entire structure was being hollowed out.
